results.text_en:
A global media company is leveraging GenAI for marketing content creation. The legal team has identified four problematic situations:

[Identified Situations]
• Situation 1: AI-generated advertisement images bear striking similarities in composition and style to work by a renowned photographer
• Situation 2: AI generates comparative advertising copy that falsely disparages a competitor's product performance (containing unverified facts)
• Situation 3: Personal information (name, address) entered in a customer prompt is directly exposed in AI-generated marketing emails
• Situation 4: AI-generated job advertisements repeatedly use language expressing preference for a specific gender

Which of the following most accurately analyzes the legal and business risks in these situations?
results.explanation_en:
Analysis of risks in each situation:

Situation 1 - Intellectual Property (IP) Infringement Risk:
GenAI can generate content similar to copyrighted works in training data. There is risk of copyright infringement litigation, and the company may bear legal liability.

Situation 2 - Hallucination-Induced Misinformation Risk:
AI generates unverified information leading to false advertising, defamation, and violations of consumer protection laws.

Situation 3 - Personal Data Protection and End-User Risk:
Exposure of customer data in AI output results in GDPR and personal data protection law violations, plus loss of customer trust.

Situation 4 - Discriminatory Model Output and Bias Risk:
Gender-biased job advertisements violate employment discrimination laws and damage brand reputation.

These four situations represent actual legal and business risks in GenAI deployment.
results.key_points_en:
GenAI Legal and Business Risk Types:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Intellectual Property (IP) Infringement:
• Generated content similar to copyrighted training data
• Risk of copyright, trademark, patent infringement
• Mitigation: Verify training data licensing
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Hallucination-Induced Misinformation:
• Generation of unverified facts
• False advertising, defamation, consumer harm
• Mitigation: Human review, fact verification pipeline
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Personal Data Exposure (End-User Risk):
• Input data exposed in output
• GDPR and personal data protection law violations
• Mitigation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails sensitive data redaction
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Biased Output (Discrimination Risk):
• Generation of content disadvantageous to specific groups
• Violations of employment discrimination laws
• Mitigation: Amazon SageMaker Clarify for bias detection
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Customer Trust Loss:
• Brand reputation damage from AI errors
• Customer churn and business loss
• Mitigation: Human-in-the-loop oversight

options.text_en: Only Situation 1's IP infringement constitutes a legal risk; the other situations are general GenAI characteristics without legal implications
options.explanation_en: Hallucinations causing misinformation, personal data exposure, and discriminatory content all represent substantive legal risks.

options.text_en: The company bears no legal responsibility for AI-generated content since legal liability rests solely with the AI model provider (such as AWS)
options.explanation_en: Typically in AI service contracts, the user (company) bears legal responsibility for content. AWS provides infrastructure but does not guarantee content compliance.

options.text_en: All four situations represent actual GenAI legal and business risks: Situation 1-IP infringement litigation, Situation 2-hallucination-induced false advertising and defamation, Situation 3-personal data protection law violation, Situation 4-gender discrimination constituting employment discrimination law violation
options.explanation_en: All four represent genuine legal and business risks that companies face when deploying GenAI. Each involves different legal domains and compliance requirements.

options.text_en: All problems arising from GenAI use represent general AI technology limitations and therefore bear no legal responsibility
options.explanation_en: AI technology limitations do not constitute legal liability exemptions. Companies bear legal responsibility for AI system outputs.

results.text_en:
A human resources startup is developing an automated resume screening AI. The training data comprises 10 years of hiring records from five major IT companies. Data analysis reveals the following:

[Data Analysis Results]
• 85% of accepted resumes: male
• 95% of accepted resumes: age 22-35
• Only 3% of accepted resumes: minority ethnic groups
• Educational institution pattern: 78% from top 20 universities
• Previous employer size: 90% from companies with 500+ employees

Which of the following most accurately explains the problems and impact of this dataset?
results.explanation_en:
This dataset contains severe structural bias:

Problem Analysis:
1) Gender bias: 85% male → AI may systematically undervalue female applicants
2) Age bias: 95% aged 22-35 → Unfairly excludes experienced talent over 36
3) Ethnic bias: 3% minorities → Disadvantages applicants from diverse ethnic backgrounds
4) Educational bias: Preference for specific universities → Overlooks ability regardless of alma mater
5) Company size bias: Disadvantages mid-market and small company experience

Root Cause: Historical hiring decisions themselves were already biased, and AI learns and amplifies this bias.

Dataset Improvement Directions:
• Inclusivity: Incorporate candidates from diverse backgrounds
• Diversity: Ensure gender, age, ethnic, and educational diversity
• Balanced Dataset: Adjust ratios across groups
• Curation: Connect hiring outcomes to actual job performance
results.key_points_en:
Importance of Dataset Characteristics:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Inclusivity:
• Ensure underrepresented groups sufficiently included in training data
• Develop models functioning appropriately across all user types
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Diversity:
• Gender, age, ethnic, regional, and cultural diversity
• Reflect varied perspectives and experiences
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Balanced Dataset:
• Maintain appropriate ratios between classes/groups
• Imbalance → leads to majority class bias
• Techniques: Oversampling, undersampling, SMOTE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Curated Data Sources:
• Quality-validated trustworthy sources
• Remove records of biased historical decisions
• Include domain expert review
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Bias in Data Reinforcement Cycle:
Biased historical data → Biased AI model
→ Biased decision-making → More biased data generated

options.text_en: Quality issues are automatically resolved because data quantity is sufficient. 10 years of data is trustworthy
options.explanation_en: Large data quantity only reinforces biased patterns. Ten years of biased hiring decisions cause AI to learn and amplify that bias.

options.text_en: This dataset is objective because it reflects actual hiring outcomes. AI naturally learns patterns from historical data
options.explanation_en: Past hiring decisions themselves were already biased. Learning from biased historical data causes AI to perpetuate and automate that bias.

options.text_en: This dataset critically lacks inclusivity, diversity, and balance. AI risks systematically discriminating against women, older workers, minorities, non-elite university graduates, and mid-market company veterans, potentially violating employment discrimination laws
options.explanation_en: This precisely identifies severe demographic bias in the dataset and its direct consequences for discriminatory AI decision-making.

options.text_en: Data bias can be completely corrected algorithmically, so diversity in data collection is not critical
options.explanation_en: Algorithmic bias correction is limited. Fundamental data quality and representation issues must be addressed at the data collection stage.

results.text_en:
A healthcare startup operates a system using Amazon SageMaker to analyze patient EMR (electronic medical record) data and compute disease risk scores.

Each analysis request contains up to 800 MB of medical imaging and biomarker data per patient. After physicians complete patient consultations and submit analysis requests, the system begins processing, which typically requires 3-8 minutes. Physicians review results on a dashboard after completion, so immediate response is not required.

What is the most appropriate Amazon SageMaker inference method for these requirements?
results.explanation_en:
Real-time Inference uses continuously provisioned endpoints for millisecond-to-second response requirements in low-latency workloads. Since this scenario allows time to wait for results, maintaining always-on endpoints generates unnecessary computing costs.

Batch Transform is optimal for processing large datasets of tens of GB or more stored in S3 through scheduled jobs. This scenario involves individual physician-submitted single requests processed per submission, which does not match Batch Transform's large-scale batch processing model.

Serverless Inference addresses sporadic and unpredictable traffic without infrastructure management, with cost optimization as primary objective. This problem's requirements involve lengthy processing time and asynchronous result delivery, which Serverless Inference alone cannot fulfill.

Asynchronous Inference immediately queues incoming requests, processes them in the background, and stores completed results in Amazon S3. This method optimizes for scenarios with multi-minute processing duration, sub-1 GB payload sizes, and where immediate response is unnecessary. The physician workflow of submitting requests after consultation and reviewing results later on a dashboard perfectly aligns with Asynchronous Inference's design intent.
results.key_points_en:
Real-time Inference:
Continuously provisioned endpoint-based, millisecond-to-second latency for immediate response requirements

Serverless Inference:
Automatic scaling for sporadic traffic without infrastructure management, zero cost during idle periods

Batch Transform:
Offline batch processing of large datasets in S3, suitable for GB-scale data

Asynchronous Inference:
Per-request under 1 GB, multi-minute processing acceptable, results stored in S3 for later retrieval

options.text_en: Real-time Inference
options.explanation_en: Real-time Inference targets low-latency workloads requiring millisecond-to-second response through always-provisioned endpoints. Maintaining always-on endpoints for this scenario where response delay is acceptable incurs unnecessary computing costs.

options.text_en: Batch Transform
options.explanation_en: Batch Transform optimizes for large-scale datasets of tens of GB or more stored in S3, processed through scheduled jobs. This scenario involves per-request processing from individual physicians, which does not align with Batch Transform's large-scale batch processing model.

options.text_en: Serverless Inference
options.explanation_en: Serverless Inference addresses sporadic and unpredictable traffic with cost optimization as the primary objective. This problem's requirements involve extended processing time and asynchronous result delivery, which Serverless Inference alone cannot fulfill.

options.text_en: Asynchronous Inference
options.explanation_en: Asynchronous Inference immediately enqueues incoming requests and processes them in the background, storing completed results in Amazon S3. This optimizes for scenarios with multi-minute processing duration, sub-1 GB payload sizes, and where immediate response is unnecessary. The physician workflow—submitting requests post-consultation and reviewing results later via dashboard—precisely matches Asynchronous Inference's design intent.

results.text_en:
A bank deployed a machine learning model for consumer loan approval and observed the following performance data:

[Model Performance Data]
• Training data accuracy: 97%
• Validation data accuracy: 73%
• Urban resident prediction accuracy: 91%
• Rural resident prediction accuracy: 64%
• Age 25-40 group prediction accuracy: 89%
• Age 55+ group prediction accuracy: 61%
• Male applicant loan approval rate: 72%
• Female applicant approval rate (identical credit score): 58%

Which of the following most accurately analyzes this model's problems and impact?
results.explanation_en:
This model exhibits two critical simultaneous issues:

1) Overfitting:
The 24% gap between training accuracy (97%) and validation accuracy (73%) indicates the model memorized training data and fails to generalize to new data. The model learned both signal and noise in training data.

2) Demographic Bias:
• Geographic bias: Rural 64% vs. urban 91% (27% gap) → Rural residents receive inaccurate approval decisions
• Age bias: Age 55+ at 61% vs. age 25-40 at 89% (28% gap) → Older adults face financial service access disadvantages
• Gender bias: Identical credit scores yield different approval rates—women 58% vs. men 72% → Clear gender discrimination

These biases create risk of violating financial service anti-discrimination laws (U.S. ECOA, domestic credit information laws, etc.)
results.key_points_en:
Bias-Variance Tradeoff:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Overfitting (High Variance, Low Bias):
• Symptom: High training accuracy, lower validation/test accuracy (large gap)
• Cause: Model too complex, memorizes training data
• Solution: Regularization, dropout, more training data, early stopping
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Underfitting (High Bias, Low Variance):
• Symptom: Both training and validation accuracy low
• Cause: Model too simple, fails to capture data patterns
• Solution: More complex model, feature engineering, extended training
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Demographic Bias Impact:
• Disadvantage specific groups: Restricted service access
• Legal risk: Potential discrimination law violations
• Social inequality: AI reinforces existing disparities
• Business risk: Regulatory penalties, litigation, trust loss
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Subgroup Analysis:
• Separately measure performance across demographic groups
• Amazon SageMaker Clarify: Automated subgroup bias analysis
• Add fairness constraints for equitable performance

options.text_en: Model performance is excellent since training accuracy reaches 97%. Group performance differences reflect natural dataset characteristics
options.explanation_en: Focusing only on training accuracy is misleading. Validation accuracy drops to 73%, indicating clear overfitting. Group performance gaps represent discrimination, not natural data properties.

options.text_en: This model exhibits underfitting. Using a more complex model architecture would resolve it
options.explanation_en: Underfitting occurs when both training and validation accuracy are low. This model shows overfitting with the 24% accuracy gap between training (97%) and validation (73%).

options.text_en: This model exhibits overfitting (training 97% vs. validation 73%) and severe demographic bias (performance gaps by geography, age, gender). Rural residents, older adults, and women receive inaccurate loan approvals, creating systematic disadvantage in financial services
options.explanation_en: This precisely diagnoses the combined problems of overfitting and multiple demographic biases, and accurately describes the business and social impact.

options.text_en: Group performance differences reflect actual credit risk differences for those groups and therefore do not constitute bias
options.explanation_en: Different approval rates for identical credit scores based on gender represents discrimination unrelated to actual credit risk. This constitutes illegal discrimination under financial regulations.

results.text_en:
A healthcare robotics company seeks to enhance surgical assistive robot decision-making capabilities. The robot must in real-time adjust medical instrument movement trajectories and select the safest path for the patient from multiple manipulation scenarios. The AI team wants to clarify the core conceptual differences between reinforcement learning and supervised learning to select the most appropriate machine learning approach.

Which of the following most accurately describes the core difference between reinforcement learning and supervised learning?
results.explanation_en:
Reinforcement Learning (RL) is where an agent selects actions in an environment and receives rewards or penalties for those actions, learning through trial and error which actions produce the best long-term outcomes. Rather than receiving pre-labeled correct answers, RL improves its policy (action strategy) through behavioral feedback. For surgical robots requiring action decisions with result-based improvement, reinforcement learning is appropriate.

Supervised Learning (SL) uses input-label pairs to train models for accurate predictions. Examples include classification tasks like 'this image is/is not bleeding' or regression tasks like 'in this state, apply this manipulation pressure.'
results.key_points_en:
Reinforcement Learning (RL):
Agent + Environment interaction + Reward/penalty feedback
→ Learns optimal action strategy (policy)

Supervised Learning (SL):
Input + labeled ground truth pairs
→ Learns prediction (classification/regression) models

RL Core Components:
Agent, environment, actions, states, rewards, policy—no labeled ground truth required

Use Case Comparison:
RL → Path optimization, game playing, robot control
SL → Image classification, demand forecasting, diagnosis

options.text_en: Both reinforcement learning and supervised learning require a dataset with ground truth labels to learn
options.explanation_en: Supervised learning requires labels as essential input. Reinforcement learning does not require labeled data; instead it learns from reward signals indicating action quality. Action-by-action ground truth labels are not necessary.

options.text_en: Reinforcement learning clusters unlabeled data to find patterns; supervised learning trains predictive models using labeled data
options.explanation_en: The description of clustering unlabeled data characterizes unsupervised learning (K-means, DBSCAN). Reinforcement learning's core is not clustering but agent-environment interaction with reward-based policy improvement.

options.text_en: Reinforcement learning involves agents learning optimal action strategies through environment interaction and reward/penalty feedback, while supervised learning trains input-output mapping models using labeled data
options.explanation_en: Reinforcement Learning (RL) involves agents selecting actions in environments and receiving reward/penalty feedback that drives learning of which actions produce best long-term outcomes through trial and error. Rather than pre-labeled answers, RL improves policy through behavioral feedback. For surgical robots requiring action decisions with result-based improvement, reinforcement learning applies. Supervised Learning (SL) uses input-label pairs for accurate predictions. Examples include classification like 'bleeding/no bleeding' from images, or regression like 'apply this manipulation pressure in this state.'

options.text_en: Reinforcement learning trains using labeled ground truth data; supervised learning involves agents acting, receiving rewards, and learning optimal behaviors
options.explanation_en: This completely reverses the definitions of reinforcement learning and supervised learning. Learning from labeled ground truth data describes supervised learning, while agents acting and receiving rewards describes reinforcement learning.
